planting.note: Set out grafted trees after all danger of frost; grow in a large container where winters drop below 25 F.

directions: Lisbon is a vigorous, cold-tolerant lemon that fruits heavily and produces classic tart, juicy fruit almost year-round in warm regions. Plant in full sun in fast-draining soil and keep the graft union above the soil line. In zones colder than 9, grow in a pot that can be moved indoors or to a bright greenhouse for winter. Feed with a citrus-specific fertilizer during active growth and water deeply but allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings. Thin fruit lightly on young trees so the branches are not overloaded.

planting.note: Plant after frost; this dwarf fig stays small enough for containers that can be sheltered over winter in colder zones.

directions: Little Miss Figgy is a naturally dwarf fig reaching only about 4 to 5 feet, making it ideal for patios and small gardens. It produces sweet, deep-purple figs on new growth, often with a light second crop later in the season. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and water consistently while fruit is sizing up. In zones below 7, grow it in a container and move it to an unheated garage or shed during hard freezes. Prune lightly in late winter to shape the plant and encourage fruiting wood.

planting.note: Set out after frost danger; grow in a pot where winters fall below 20 F so it can overwinter indoors.

directions: Little Ragu is a compact, upright form of culinary sweet bay with aromatic evergreen leaves used fresh or dried in soups and stews. It stays tidy at 6 to 8 feet and takes well to pruning or shaping in containers. Plant in full sun to light shade in well-drained soil and let the surface dry slightly between waterings, as it dislikes soggy roots. In cold-winter areas grow it in a pot and bring it into a cool, bright spot before hard frost. Harvest mature leaves any time and dry them for the strongest flavor.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the soil has warmed and frost has passed; lift and store tubers over winter in cold zones.

directions: Maarn is a classic orange ball dahlia that produces an abundance of round, uniform blooms perfect for cutting. The plants are large and well-balanced, often standing without staking, and they generate plenty of tubers for dividing. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after frost has passed. Cut flowers frequently and deadhead spent blooms to keep the plant producing all season. In zones colder than 8, dig and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter.

planting.note: Plant in early spring once soil is workable; elderberries fruit best with a second variety nearby for pollination.

directions: Marge is a prolific European elderberry prized for heavy yields of dark berries used in syrups, jams, and wine. Plant in full sun in moist, fertile soil and keep it well watered, as elderberries are shallow-rooted and thirsty. Fruit set improves dramatically with a second, different elderberry variety planted nearby for cross-pollination. Prune out the oldest canes each late winter to keep the plant vigorous and productive. Always cook the berries before eating, since raw fruit and other plant parts can cause stomach upset.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in humus-rich, well-drained soil; grown mainly for its foliage.

directions: Marmalade is a coral bells cultivar grown for its ruffled amber, caramel, and rose-tinged foliage that holds color through the seasons. It forms a neat mound and sends up airy flower spikes that attract hummingbirds. Plant in partial shade in rich, well-drained soil, keeping the crown at soil level to prevent rot. Water evenly during dry spells and mulch lightly to keep roots cool. Divide clumps every few years in spring to keep the plant vigorous and refresh its color.

planting.note: Set out after frost; treated as an annual in cold zones and a woody perennial in zones 7 and warmer.

directions: Mary Ann is a heat- and drought-tolerant lantana bearing clusters of yellow, orange, and purple-pink flowers from spring through fall. The blooms are a magnet for butterflies and other pollinators while shrugging off humidity and poor soil. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and water sparingly once established, as it thrives on neglect. In zones colder than 7 it is grown as an annual or overwintered indoors. Trim back leggy stems midseason to keep the plant compact and flowering freely.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in part shade.

directions: Mighty Chocolate Cherry is a tall astilbe with dramatic cherry-red plumes rising above green-burgundy chocolate-toned foliage. It brings bold vertical color to shaded, moist borders and woodland gardens. Plant in partial shade in rich soil that stays evenly moist, since astilbe suffers if it dries out. Water regularly and mulch to conserve moisture and keep roots cool. Cut back spent foliage in late fall and divide clumps every three to four years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant only where winters stay above freezing; young trees are very frost-sensitive and benefit from wind protection.

directions: Mauritius is a productive lychee bearing rough-skinned red fruit with sweet, fragrant, translucent flesh. It needs a frost-free subtropical or tropical climate with a brief cool, dry period to trigger flowering. Plant in full sun in deep, acidic, well-drained soil and protect young trees from wind and cold. Water generously during flowering and fruit development but ease off during the winter rest period. Mulch heavily to keep the shallow roots moist and feed with a balanced fertilizer through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant after frost; grow in a container in zones colder than 8 so it can shelter indoors over winter.

directions: The olive is a slow-growing Mediterranean evergreen with silvery foliage, gnarled character, and small fruit ripening from green to black. It thrives in hot, dry conditions and needs a stretch of winter chill to set fruit well. Plant in full sun in fast-draining, even rocky soil and water deeply but infrequently once established. In zones below 8, grow it in a large pot and move it to a cool, bright indoor space during hard freezes. Prune in late winter to open the canopy and cure the fruit before eating, as fresh olives are bitter.

planting.note: Sow early; soaking seed overnight speeds notoriously slow germination.

directions: Italian Giant is a robust flat-leaved parsley prized by cooks for its strong, fresh flavor and easy-to-chop leaves. Grown as an annual, it produces large, dark green leaves ideal for soups, stews, and garnishes. Sow seed directly or start indoors, keeping the soil consistently moist since germination is slow and can take up to three weeks. Give it full sun to part shade and harvest outer stems first so the plant keeps producing. It tolerates light frost and can be grown in containers on a sunny windowsill.

planting.note: Plant after frost danger; grow in a container where winters drop below 30 F.

directions: Persian Bearss is a prolific, thornless lime producing juicy, seedless fruit perfect for cooking and drinks. It is one of the more cold-tolerant limes but still needs protection below freezing. Plant in full sun in fast-draining soil and keep the graft union above the soil line. Water deeply and let the top inch of soil dry between waterings, feeding with a citrus fertilizer during active growth. In zones colder than 9, grow in a large pot that can move to a bright indoor location for winter.

planting.note: Plant after frost; ideal as a container tree that overwinters indoors in cold climates.

directions: Ponderosa is a lemon-citron hybrid famous for enormous, grapefruit-sized, thick-skinned fruit with tart, seedy flesh. It is compact and free-fruiting, making it a rewarding container tree for patios and sunrooms. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and keep the graft union above the soil surface. Water deeply, letting the surface dry between waterings, and feed with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season. In zones colder than 9, bring the pot indoors to a bright spot before frost arrives.
